report:
FITNESS GOAL ANALYSIS REPORT
Goal Period: March 2 – May 16, 2026 (76 days)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1. GOAL OUTCOME

1 of 2 goals met.

Goal Met: 1 of 2 | Weight: lost 16.1 lbs (16.1 lbs vs 9.3 lbs target) MET | Body Fat: lost 3.91% (ending at 15.57% vs 15.0% target) MISSED

The weight goal was exceeded by a significant margin — you lost 73% more than targeted. The body fat goal came close but fell just short, ending at 15.57% versus the 15.0% target. The 0.57% gap is directionally meaningful but relatively narrow; BF% measurement precision should be held in mind when evaluating this miss (see BF% notes in Section 2).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

2. THE NUMBERS

Start Weight:            139.3 lbs
End Weight:              123.2 lbs
Weight Lost:             16.1 lbs (target: 9.3 lbs)

Start BF%:               19.48%
End BF%:                 15.57%
BF% Lost:                3.91% (target: reach 15.0%)
Note: BF% figures are based on weekly rolling averages applied to daily readings. With only 11 weekly datapoints, directional trends are reliable but the exact endpoint value carries meaningful measurement uncertainty. The 0.57% miss may be within device margin of error.

Start Lean Mass:         112.2 lbs
End Lean Mass:           104.0 lbs
Lean Mass Lost:          8.2 lbs

Avg Daily Calories:      1,432 (target: 1,442) — 99.3% adherence
Calorie Logging:         92% of days logged
Deficit Adherence:       Excellent on average; high variance day-to-day (range: 539–3,416 kcal)

Avg Daily Protein:       130.6g (target: 135g; floor: 108g)
Protein at/above target: 50% of logged days
Protein at/above floor:  83% of logged days

Total Workout Sessions:  54 over 76 days
Avg Sessions per Week:   5.0
Avg Strain Score:        18.33

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

3. PROJECTION (GOALS MISSED)

Body fat target: 15.0%
Current endpoint: 15.57%
Remaining gap: 0.57%

The rolling 7-day BF% trend in the final three weeks of the goal averaged roughly 0.32–0.36% reduction per week. At that rate, the 15.0% target would be reached in approximately 1.5 to 2 additional weeks of similar input consistency.

Important caveat: fat loss is rarely linear, and at lower body fat percentages the rate of measurable change tends to slow. The 1.5–2 week estimate assumes current calorie, protein, and training inputs are maintained. If the recent plateau tendency seen in weeks 9–11 (weight stabilizing in the 125–126 lb range) continues, the timeline could stretch to 3 weeks.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

4. WHAT DROVE RESULTS

The dominant input driving weight loss was sustained caloric deficit across the full 76-day period. Average daily intake of 1,432 calories against presumably a higher total daily energy expenditure (supported by consistent strain scores averaging 18.33, including frequent running and weightlifting) created the energy deficit responsible for most of the 16.1 lb loss.

Several specific windows stand out:

Early acceleration (March 2–25): Weight dropped from 139.3 to 131.2 lbs — a loss of 8.1 lbs in roughly three weeks. This occurred despite the first week having no logged calories and the second week (March 9–15) showing the worst protein adherence of the entire goal period (weekly average 94.1g, well below the 108g floor). The loss during this phase appears to be driven heavily by high training volume (strain scores consistently 17–21) and what was likely a meaningful caloric deficit even in unlogged days. Some portion of early loss — likely 2–4 lbs — was water weight associated with reduced carbohydrate intake and increased exercise.

Stabilization and slower progress (March 25 – April 13): Weight plateaued roughly in the 131–133 lb range for about three weeks. During this window, calorie intake was being logged consistently but averaged 1,384–1,410 kcal — close to target. The BF% rolling average continued declining (18.36 → 17.80), suggesting body composition was still improving even as scale weight stalled. This is consistent with water retention masking fat loss, particularly with high training loads.

Renewed acceleration (April 13 – April 28): Weight dropped from 131.2 to 127.2 lbs — 4.0 lbs in about two weeks. Looking back 3–7 days before this shift, protein intake rose noticeably: weekly average protein climbed from 131.6g (week of March 30) to 140.1g (week of April 6) to 141.6g (week of April 13). By April 7–9, daily protein was consistently above 150g (154.8g, 153.7g, 156.6g). This elevated protein intake, coinciding with calorie intake remaining controlled, likely supported better muscle retention and may have contributed to a slightly higher thermic effect of food, facilitating the resumed weight loss that became visible around April 13–17. The BF% rolling average also dropped more steeply during this window (17.80 → 16.84 in two weeks), directionally suggesting fat was being lost rather than lean mass — though this conclusion should be treated cautiously given BF% measurement limitations.

Final phase steady decline (April 28 – May 16): Weight moved from 127.2 to 123.2 lbs over the final 18 days, averaging roughly 0.22 lbs/day. Protein remained elevated (weekly averages 144–151g), calorie logging was consistent, and strain scores remained high. This phase showed the most controlled and consistent execution of the entire goal period.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

5. WHAT HELD YOU BACK

Lean mass loss: The most significant concern this data surfaces is the 8.2 lb lean mass loss alongside 16.1 lbs of total weight loss. That is a lean-to-fat loss ratio of roughly 51:49 — meaning approximately half of all weight lost appears to have come from lean tissue. For a goal emphasizing body recomposition and a 15% BF target, this is a notable inefficiency. The primary likely contributors, based on the data:

Chronically under-target protein in the first half: For the first five weeks (March 2 through roughly April 6), protein averaged between 94g and 131g per day on a weekly basis. The 108g floor was only met 83% of days across the full period, meaning floor misses were concentrated heavily in the early weeks. Low protein during a caloric deficit is the most direct input associated with lean mass loss in this dataset.

Extreme low-calorie days: Several days logged intake below 800 calories (March 12: 777 kcal, March 16: 962 kcal, March 24: 539 kcal, April 1: 934 kcal). A 539-calorie day is severe. These days are likely associated with muscle catabolism, especially when protein was also low on those same days (March 12: 53g; March 24: 68.5g). These are the single worst individual data points in the entire period from a lean mass preservation standpoint.

BF% goal narrowly missed: The BF% target of 15.0% was not reached, ending at 15.57%. Given the lean mass loss noted above, the BF% outcome was partially determined by losing both fat and muscle simultaneously rather than fat alone — a more favorable lean mass outcome would likely have cleared the 15.0% target even with the same total weight loss.

Logging gaps in early weeks: The first seven days of the goal period had no calorie or protein data. This creates a blind spot during a critical establishment phase and means inputs cannot be evaluated causally for that window.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

6. MEAL PATTERNS

Calorie variance was the defining meal pattern issue. While average daily calories (1,432) tracked almost exactly to target (1,442), the day-to-day range was extreme: from 539 kcal to 3,416 kcal. This boom-bust pattern appeared in identifiable clusters:

High-calorie day followed by sharp restriction: March 13 (1,928 kcal) → March 14 (3,416 kcal) → March 15 (1,052 kcal) → March 16 (962 kcal). The 3,416-calorie day on March 14 is the single highest intake of the period and was immediately followed by two days of significant under-eating. This pattern suggests compensatory restriction after high-intake days rather than steady adherence. A similar cluster occurred around March 21–22 (2,137 kcal) followed by March 24 (539 kcal).

Sub-1,000 calorie days: There were at least six days with intake below 1,000 calories (March 12, 15, 16, 24; April 1, 6). These are not meaningful calorie targets — they are suppressed eating days, whether intentional or the result of appetite suppression or schedule disruption. These days correlate with the lowest protein readings in the dataset and are the most likely mechanism behind lean mass loss.

Protein inconsistency tracked closely with calorie inconsistency: On the days with the lowest calorie intake, protein almost universally fell below the 108g floor (March 12: 53g; March 24: 68.5g; March 10: 74.6g). This is expected, but it means the days of most aggressive restriction were also the days most harmful to muscle retention.

Logging gaps: Calories were unlogged for the first 6 days (March 2–7) and workout strain was missing on several days. The 92% logging rate is strong overall, but front-loaded gaps reduce the causal clarity of the early phase analysis.

The final five weeks (April 14 onward) showed markedly more consistent calorie logging and more stable daily intake, which aligns with the more consistent weight trajectory in that period.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

7. WORKOUT PATTERNS

Consistency was a clear strength. Five sessions per week was maintained across 10 of 11 weeks, dropping only in the final week (4 sessions). Total of 54 sessions across 76 days is high adherence for an 11-week goal.

Strain: Average strain of 18.33 is substantial and was maintained throughout. Notable spikes included April 10 (24.05), March 27 (23.03), and multiple days in the 21+ range. There were no extended low-strain stretches, suggesting no deload periods or significant training disruptions.

Sport mix: Running (54 sessions) and weightlifting (43 sessions) were the consistent anchors — note that session counts exceed total sessions because multiple activities were likely logged on the same day. Basketball appeared twice, plus one session each of HIIT, walking, and pilates. The combination of running (caloric expenditure) and weightlifting (muscle preservation stimulus) was structurally appropriate for the body recomposition goal, even if the lean mass outcome suggests nutritional inputs didn't fully support the lifting volume.

Strain and lean mass: The sustained high strain across the period — in the context of repeatedly low protein and severely under-target calorie days — may have paradoxically contributed to lean mass loss. High training stress without adequate protein on the same or adjacent days creates a catabolic environment. This is not a reason to train less; it is a reason to ensure protein floors are non-negotiable on high-strain days specifically.

Final week strain was lower (13.21 and 15.39 on May 15–16), possibly a natural taper at the goal endpoint.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

8. LESSONS FOR NEXT GOAL

The following are derived exclusively from patterns observed in this data:

Enforce the protein floor on low-calorie days specifically. The data shows a direct and consistent pattern: your lowest-protein days were your lowest-calorie days. In your next goal, treat 108g protein as a constraint that does not move regardless of total calories. If you eat 700 calories in a day, 108g of that must be protein. This single change would address the primary mechanism behind the 8.2 lbs of lean mass loss.

Eliminate sub-800 calorie days. Days at 539, 777, and 962 calories appeared multiple times and contributed nothing positive to the goal outcome. They appear to be reactive low-intake days following high-calorie days. A hard floor of 1,000–1,100 calories on any logged day would be a concrete, manageable constraint based on the range you actually experienced.

Don't compensate for high-calorie days with aggressive restriction the next day. The March 14 (3,416 kcal) to March 15–16 (1,052 / 962 kcal) sequence is the clearest example. Your weekly average still landed near target, meaning the math worked — but the extreme swings did metabolic and muscular damage the averages don't capture. In the next goal, set a ceiling of ~2,000 calories per day rather than restricting the following day.

Start logging from Day 1. The first week had zero nutrition data. Given that the early phase is when water weight, glycogen depletion, and actual fat loss are most intertwined and hardest to interpret, having that data would improve both in-goal decision-making and post-goal analysis.

The final five weeks of this goal (April 14 – May 16) are the template. During this period: logging was consistent, protein averaged 136–152g per week, calories stayed in a tighter range, and weight dropped steadily. Replicating those conditions from the start of the next goal — rather than arriving at them by week 6 — would produce faster compositional results with less lean mass loss.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
